---
title: "Northern Sierra placer: data + model overview"
subtitle: "Per-feature decisions, the v3 redesign mechanics, the empirical defense of the spatial-CV setup"
---

[← back to chapter](../../placer.html)

## What this is

The depth notebook behind [Chapter 4](../../placer.html). The chapter
covers the v3 deliverable, the v2 leakage discovery, and the ablation
that measures how much of v3's headline AUC is real Tertiary-belt
signal vs residual buffered-pit shortcut. This notebook unpacks the
per-feature design choices that lead to those results, the three
concrete mechanics that distinguish v3 from v2, and the empirical
basis for the 20 km spatial-block CV the chapter assumes.

## The Phase 1 feature stack, decision by decision

![The Phase 1 weighted-knowledge-driven placer-Au prospectivity index, on a 250 m grid. All seven anchor hydraulic-mining districts land in the top decile, with percentile ranks between 0.98 and 0.99 on their pit-centroid cells. The eastern half of the AOI is mostly zero because the Mother Lode lode-Au sources concentrate west, and the geomorphic features that score Tertiary placers fade east of the lode trend. The index is the gate every supervised pass below this layer has to clear.](../../data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/phase1_score_map.png)

**Two distance-to-lode features, not one.** The flow-routed
`distance_downstream_from_lode_m` walks the NHD network downstream
from each MRDS lode-Au seed via `hydroseq` ordering. It carries the
correct physics for modern-channel placers (gold flows downhill on real
streams), and it is NaN at 98.3% of cells in this AOI because the
Sierra Tertiary deep-gravels are paleo-channels offset from the modern
Mother Lode trend. The omnidirectional `distance_to_lode_m` companion
fires at every cell within 25 km of any lode seed regardless of
routing, which is the feature the Tertiary anchors key on.

**Tertiary terrace, three-component geometric mean.**
`tertiary_terrace_likelihood` is the geometric mean of three
components: TPI-high (the cell sits above its local neighborhood),
slope-low (bench geometry, not canyon wall), and not-Quaternary-alluvium
(the antithesis signal). I picked geometric mean over arithmetic
because any single zero signature should zero the composite. A
Tertiary terrace candidate needs all three signatures together, not
one out of three. Anchor districts that the paleochannel composite
alone misses (anchor values 0.17 to 0.44 against a raster median of
0.40) rank in the top decile on this single feature.

![The `tertiary_terrace_likelihood` feature: geometric mean of TPI-high, slope-low, and not-Quaternary-alluvium. The hot YlOrBr bands cluster in the upper-left where the dark-blue hydraulic-pit polygons sit, which is the design intent. The geometric mean zeros out anywhere any single signature is missing, so a candidate Tertiary terrace needs all three present.](../../data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/tertiary_terrace_likelihood_map.png)

**Hydraulic-pit polygons as the Tertiary seed, not MRDS placer
points.** MRDS `dep_type` tagging for placer is explicit on only 1.7%
of records in the AOI; the rest are tagged generically or not at all.
The Orlando 2016 hydraulic-mine pit polygons are the literal physical
evidence of where Tertiary pay was rich enough to justify hydraulic
mining. 167 polygons resolve to 158 unique cells at 250 m.

**Catchment-Au Hawkes, dual decay at 2 km and 15 km.** The Hawkes 1976
dual-decay kernel aggregates NGDB + NURE stream-sediment Au into each
cell's upstream catchment. Short decay (2 km) captures source-confidence
near the cell; long decay (15 km) captures regional dilution. The
20 km spatial-block CV picks block size against this long-decay
characteristic length so a test-fold cell never sits inside the
train-fold cells' aggregation kernel.

## The v3 redesign, three concrete mechanics

**Per-population feature splits.** v2 trained both populations on the
same 44-feature stack. SPI / `ksn` / Hawkes pathfinder features help
Quaternary modern-channel placers (stream-power transitions where gold
deposits) but are noise on Tertiary terraces (high above the modern
drainage). v3 splits the stack: 17 features for Tertiary
(`tertiary_terrace_likelihood`, buffered pit-proximity, `gravity`,
`distance_to_lode_m`, lithology, and others), 22 for Quaternary
(`distance_downstream_from_lode_m`, four NHD ValueAddedAttributes,
`catchment_au_hawkes`, `spi_band`, and others).

**Gaussian falloff, not hard cap.** v2's distance features used a hard
cap: cells beyond 15 km got score 0 and cells at the boundary got an
arbitrary near-zero. The score map showed a visible discontinuity at
the cap. v3 uses a gaussian falloff with a sigma_m parameter, decaying
from 1 at distance 0 to ~5% at distance 3σ. Forest Hill sits about
12 km from the nearest mapped lode seed; v2 scored it ~0.2 (just above
the hard zero), v3 with σ = 12 km scores it ~0.61 (matching its
recognized geology).

![v2's hard-cap inverse falloff (dashed grey) vs v3's gaussian falloff at σ = 12 km (solid steelblue). Forest Hill at 12 km scores ~0.2 under v2 (just above the hard zero) and ~0.61 under v3. The hard cap created a visible discontinuity in the score map at 15 km; the gaussian decays smoothly to ~5% at 3σ.](../../data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/gaussian_vs_hard_cap_falloff.png)

**Buffered pit-proximity.** v2's `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m` trivially
encoded the Tertiary label: positive cells are pit polygon centroids,
so the feature at distance 0 IS the label. v3 trains on
`hydraulic_pit_proximity_m_buffered`, which is NaN within 1 km of any
pit polygon. The buffer voids 10,000 to 20,000 cells around pits. The
model's view of "near a pit" becomes "I have no signal here", which
forces it to use the other 16 features to rank those cells. The
chapter covers the ablation that measures how much shortcut the 1 km
buffer still admits (RF drops 10.5 pp, XGB drops 11.8 pp when the
feature is dropped entirely); v3.5 widens the buffer to 2 km to
partition real signal from residual shortcut.

## Spatial-block CV, defended empirically

**Block size from the Hawkes characteristic length.** 20 km is a
little more than 2x the Hawkes long-decay (15 km), the floor at which
a test-fold cell stops sitting inside the train-fold cells'
aggregation kernel. The pre-flight diagnostic measured 8-neighbour
adjacency correlation of positive counts at 10, 15, 20, 25, and
30 km block sizes. Adjacency correlation falls 0.83 (at 10 km, adjacent
blocks are near-perfectly correlated, which defeats spatial CV's
point) to 0.46 (at 30 km, adjacent blocks are decorrelated). 20 km
sits at 0.71, 25 km at 0.57. v3 default is 20 km for compatibility
with v2; v3.5 will try 25 km.

![The spatial-CV block-size trade-off measured empirically. Left panel: 8-neighbour adjacency correlation of positive counts vs block size; the dashed grey reference line marks the 0.5 threshold above which adjacent blocks contaminate each other. Right panel: n_folds (steelblue, left axis) vs zero-positive blocks (darkorange, right axis). Smaller blocks have more folds but adjacent ones correlate; larger blocks decorrelate but waste statistical power on zero-positive blocks.](../../data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/block_size_adjacency_diagnostic.png)

**The stacking meta uses the same iterable.** The trap inside
`StackingClassifier` is that its `cv` argument silently defaults to
random KFold if you don't pass an iterable. The meta-learner then
trains on out-of-fold base predictions that came from random splits,
which leak across spatial blocks. v3 passes the same `SpatialBlockCV`
iterable to RF, LightGBM, XGBoost, AND the stacking meta's `cv`
parameter.

## Validation diagnostics that work and ones that don't

**Pearson correlation is the wrong instrument at 0.02% positive
rate.** I built a leakage-risk audit that computes Pearson and
Spearman correlation between every suspect feature and each
population's binary label, with a flag at |r| > 0.10. It flagged
nothing in v2 and nothing in v3. The v2 pit-proximity leak (RF AUC
0.995 vs LightGBM 0.804) was completely invisible to it. At a 0.02%
positive rate, the linear correlation of any feature with the binary
label has a ceiling near 0.05 just from the base rate, even for a
perfectly predictive feature. The real diagnostics are the SHAP
top-5 recipe match (which fired on `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m_buffered`
as #1 in v3 and prompted the ablation) and the held-out Lindgren PP73
secondary anchors (81.8% top quintile on the Tertiary calibrated
raster, the gate v2 failed).

**Isotonic beats Platt 100x in ECE at this base rate.** Pre-isotonic
ECE on the v3 Tertiary stacking OOF was 0.0002; post-isotonic ECE is
0.0002. Isotonic doesn't move it because the input stack arrives
well-calibrated. The Platt vs isotonic ablation shows top-decile
precision is identical (both calibrators rank the same cells as
positives), but isotonic's probability scale matches observed
frequencies more closely.

## Where to look

[← back to chapter](../../placer.html) for the v2-to-v3 narrative,
held-out validation gates, and ablation results.

The v3 phase-by-phase review notebooks (A through E) live alongside
this overview in the repo but are not in the public render. They were
review artifacts during the build, not reader-facing material.
